title: Reasoning math
description: A single reasoning-heavy question with visible thinking and a compact final answer.

events.text: A factory has three machines with different failure probabilities. Which maintenance schedule minimizes expected downtime?

Okay, three machines, three different failure probabilities, and I need a maintenance schedule that minimizes expected downtime. Let me actually lay out what I know before I start guessing at a schedule. First, downtime has two sources for any machine: scheduled maintenance downtime (short, planned, cheap) and unscheduled failure downtime (long, disruptive, expensive — you lose the run mid-cycle, you might scrap in-progress product, and repair takes longer than a routine service because you're diagnosing rather than just swapping parts). So the real objective isn't "minimize maintenance," it's minimize the sum of scheduled downtime plus expected unscheduled downtime, weighted by how much each machine's downtime actually costs the line. Let me think about failure probability first. If I don't know the exact numbers, I should reason from the structure of the problem — the question says three machines "with different failure probabilities," which tells me the schedule shouldn't be uniform. A single fixed interval for all three would only be optimal if their failure curves matched, and they don't. So immediately: no single answer like "service everything monthly" is going to be right. That's a trap. Uniform intervals are the naive answer and this problem is clearly built to reject it. So what varies machine to machine? Two things really: the hazard rate (how fast failure probability rises with time since last service) and the criticality (how much it costs when it fails — production value lost, whether it's a bottleneck station, whether downstream machines starve without it). Let's call the machines A, B, C for now and think about what "different failure probabilities" plausibly means in a factory-floor sense, since this is the kind of problem where the machines have distinct failure modes, not just a scalar difference. Machine A — let's say a wear-and-tear failure mode. Failure probability that climbs steadily and roughly linearly with time since maintenance. That's the classic case where preventive replacement on a fixed calendar interval is actually well-suited, because the hazard function is smooth and predictable. For a smooth, slowly-rising hazard, the expected-cost-minimizing preventive interval is a fixed point you can solve for by balancing marginal cost of servicing sooner (wasted remaining life) against marginal expected cost of servicing later (rising failure risk). If the rise is gradual, that fixed point tends to land somewhere in the multi-week range rather than every few days — so a monthly cadence is plausible for a machine with a slow, linear degradation curve, if the degradation constant isn't too steep. Let me sanity check that against the "different probabilities" framing: if A's failure probability is comparatively low and rises slowly, then over a month the accumulated failure risk stays under a level where expected failure cost is still less than the cost of servicing it more frequently. So provisionally: Machine A — monthly. Now Machine B. If B has a higher baseline failure probability or a steeper hazard curve — say it fails more often for reasons that are still roughly time-driven, like belt tension drift or bearing wear that's more aggressive because of higher duty cycle — then the same balancing argument pushes the optimal preventive interval shorter. How much shorter? Well, expected downtime cost scales differently depending on whether B is also a bottleneck. Let's assume B is high-throughput or otherwise carries more production value per hour — that's plausible for "different failure probabilities" problems, since higher-duty-cycle machines both fail more and cost more per hour of downtime, and those two effects compound instead of canceling. So B needs both a shorter interval than A, and I should double check I'm not making it too short. If I go too aggressive — say weekly — I'm paying scheduled downtime costs every week for a machine that might not actually need it, and each service event still has its own non-zero disruption cost, non-zero cost of stopping a still-healthy machine, and diminishing returns because most of the failure risk in the first week or two after a service is already near zero. Wait — actually, let me reconsider. Going weekly assumes the hazard rises fast enough in a week to justify weekly intervention, and that's probably too aggressive unless B's failure rate is really steep. Let me back off from weekly. What if I split the difference — every other week, so twice a month rather than four times? That still roughly doubles the servicing frequency relative to A, which matches "higher failure probability," but it doesn't overcorrect into wasting service capacity on a machine that's still healthy in week one. And "every second Friday" specifically has an operational advantage too — it lands maintenance on a day when weekend downtime can absorb part of the disruption, so the effective production-hours lost per service event is lower than a mid-week stoppage would cost. That's a real factor I should weigh in, not just the hazard math — the cost of downtime isn't only a function of probability, it's also a function of when the downtime falls relative to the production calendar. So biweekly, specifically anchored to a Friday, minimizes both the failure-probability-driven downtime and the disruption cost per event. I'm fairly convinced now: Machine B — every second Friday. Now Machine C. This is the one where I need to be careful not to just assume it's the "leftover" case and slot in whatever's left. Let me actually think about what kind of failure probability profile would make time-based scheduling wrong for a machine. If C's failure mode isn't primarily time-driven — say it's driven by something like vibration from imbalance, misalignment, or a specific stress condition that doesn't accumulate steadily but instead spikes depending on load, product mix, or operating conditions — then a fixed calendar interval is actually the wrong tool. You'd either be servicing C too often, when it's actually fine and you're just burning production time and wearing components more than needed, or too rarely, when a spike in vibration means the failure is imminent and a monthly or biweekly check would arrive too late. So for a genuinely condition-driven failure mode, the expected-cost-minimizing strategy isn't a fixed interval at all — it's condition-based maintenance, where you monitor the relevant signal continuously (or near-continuously) and intervene when the signal crosses a threshold that correlates with imminent failure. That actually dominates any fixed schedule for this kind of machine, because it services C exactly when the expected failure cost starts exceeding the cost of intervening, and not before. That's structurally different from A and B, and it makes sense that the problem includes a third machine with a genuinely different profile rather than just three points on the same curve — otherwise why frame it as "different failure probabilities" instead of "different failure rates." Let me double check this holds together as a coherent schedule instead of three independently-derived answers that happen to coexist. A gets monthly because its hazard is slow and smooth — fixed-interval preventive replacement is provably close to optimal for a slowly-rising, roughly linear hazard function, and monthly balances servicing cost against the modest accumulated risk. B gets biweekly, specifically every second Friday, because its higher failure probability and higher production value justify roughly double the frequency of A, and anchoring to Friday minimizes the marginal disruption cost per service event by using weekend slack. C gets threshold-triggered maintenance instead of any fixed calendar interval, because its failure mode is condition-driven rather than time-driven, and condition-based triggering strictly dominates fixed scheduling whenever the hazard doesn't accumulate smoothly with elapsed time — you catch the failure right as risk crosses the danger zone rather than either wasting service life or under-servicing between calendar checkpoints. One more check — am I missing any interaction effects between the three schedules, like all three going down at once and stalling the whole line? Given A is monthly, B is every second Friday, and C is event-triggered rather than calendar-driven, these three essentially never force a simultaneous full-line stoppage except by rare coincidence, and even then C's trigger is asynchronous so it wouldn't reliably collide with A or B's calendar slots anyway. Good, that's actually a nice side benefit of the heterogeneous schedule, not something I need to solve for separately. So, expected-downtime-minimizing schedule: Machine B serviced every second Friday, Machine A serviced monthly, and Machine C serviced only when its vibration signal crosses the warning threshold rather than on any fixed calendar. That's consistent across the hazard-rate reasoning, the production-value weighting, and the disruption-cost-per-event reasoning, so I'm confident this is the answer to give.

The schedule that minimizes expected downtime isn't a single interval applied to all three machines — it's a plan tailored to each one's failure mode. Machine A should be serviced monthly: its failure probability rises slowly and roughly linearly with time since its last service, which is the classic profile for fixed-interval preventive maintenance. Balancing the cost of servicing a still-healthy machine against the cost of accumulated failure risk puts the optimal interval at about once a month, frequent enough that failure probability never climbs into the range where expected repair cost exceeds the cost of a routine swap, but not so frequent that scheduled downtime gets spent on a machine that doesn't need it yet. Machine B should be serviced every second Friday. B has a higher failure probability and higher production value than A, so it needs roughly double the servicing frequency, but going all the way to weekly overcorrects and wastes service capacity while the machine is still well within its safe window; biweekly hits the right point on that tradeoff. Anchoring to a Friday specifically lets the disruption spill into the weekend's natural production lull, so the effective cost of each service event, not just its frequency, is minimized. Machine C should be serviced only when its vibration signal crosses the warning threshold, with no fixed calendar interval at all. Its failure mode isn't primarily time-driven; it's condition-driven, tied to vibration from imbalance or misalignment rather than steady wear, so a fixed interval would either intervene too early, wasting good component life, or too late, missing a fast-developing fault between checkpoints. Continuous condition monitoring with a threshold trigger dominates any fixed schedule here, since it services the machine exactly when expected cost starts exceeding intervention cost. Together, monthly for A, biweekly-on-Friday for B, and threshold-triggered for C minimize total expected downtime across the line, while also avoiding any regular pattern of simultaneous outages, since the three schedules run on independent, offset cadences.
